A police officer told me that once your car is stolen from Nairobi (CBD), pray that it does not take the Thika Road route... In 15 or 30 minutes, it will either be in Thika or Kiambu, and within the next 30 or 1 hour, either dismantled or number plate changed.

He said you will always have just one hour of hoping your car is found if it takes the route. Those posh houses in these locations are run by these thieves, not just a shanty garage.

He said proboxes are rarely dismantled but number plates changed and sold to Somalis in Mandera to transport goods and miraa across the border.

Back to this again.

The Ngong Road–Southern Bypass Interchange (Lenana Interchange).

This is a major full cloverleaf road junction on Nairobi's Southern Bypass, designed to enable free flowing movement between Ngong Road, Karen, Dagoretti, and the Southern Bypass without the need for traffic lights. 

Constructed as part of the 29.6 kilometre Southern Bypass project between 2013 and 2016, the interchange was built by the China Road and Bridge Corporation (CRBC) and financed by the Export-Import Bank of China under the larger Southern Bypass project, which cost approximately KSh 18.8 billion. 

UhuRuto works to open the western corridors.

Zero jam

Tweet_Text:
A mother survives the fear and trauma of almost losing her daughter in a school inferno, only to lose her own life while rushing to see that same child. 

After days of panic, prayers and sleepless nights, she was on the Nakuru–Nairobi Highway heading to embrace her daughter a survivor to comfort her, to remind her she was alive, loved and not alone.

But death intercepted her journey at Kariandusi.

Now a young girl who escaped fire and tragedy at school must face another unbearable nightmare: the mother who was coming to hold her one more time will never arrive. Her father is injured, her family shattered, and her entire world changed within moments.

No child deserves this much pain. No parent deserves such a cruel ending. Life can be unbearably merciless sometimes.

May her soul rest in peace, and may that little girl find strength through a grief too heavy for any child to carry.

Tweet_Text:
City to city connection 

The cities of Nairobi and Mombasa, besides the air and road connection, another game changer between these two cities is the Standard gauge railway (SGR).

It's a modern, Chinese built which has fundamentally reduced passenger transit times and costs.

The passenger line, named the Madaraka Express, operates between the Port of Mombasa, Nairobi, and Naivasha, covering 13 destinations including Emali, Kibwezi, Mtito, Andei and Voi.

The journey between Mombasa and Nairobi lasts about 4.5 hours .
Before you enjoy this SGR ride, prior online booking and payment is necessary and don't get surprised when you find no space available.

The trip between the 2 cities is exciting as you see lots of natural attractions; wildlife in Voi and Tsavo, beautiful landscape and more.

Today, we joined hands with Big Ship, Kenya Forest Service, Kenya Wildlife Service, Kenya Airways, KATA, our tour partners, and stakeholders in a mangrove restoration activity at Tudor Creek.

Mangroves are vital ecosystems — they protect coastlines from erosion, provide breeding grounds for fish and crabs, and capture significant amounts of carbon. By planting seedlings together, we’re contributing to the recovery of this critical habitat that supports both nature and local communities.

 It was a day of muddy hands, strong teamwork, and shared commitment to sustainability.

Tweet_Text:
Kevin Kiarie represented Kenya with dignity while the system treated him like he was invisible.

A young man wins gold for this country in Cairo, pays for his own ticket. Wins again abroad, travels like an ordinary hustler using a matatu to the airport, stands alone in Berlin after raising our flag, yet the same government rushing to post congratulatory messages could not stand with him when he needed support.

Then they hide behind “procedure” and “channels” after he already carried the Kenyan flag higher than many well-funded officials ever will. If the channels are so important, why do they only open for connected people, political loyalists and noise makers?

This country must stop punishing discipline, talent and determination. We cannot keep celebrating mediocrity and empty political theatrics while abandoning serious young sportsmen fighting for Kenya with their own sweat and money.

And let us be honest, many Kenyans are asking an uncomfortable question: would Kevin be treated this way if he came from the “right” political circles or community? Because too many hardworking youths are being frustrated while politically connected individuals enjoy state protection and endless funding despite producing nothing.

On Tuesday, fellow Kenyans, let us go to JKIA with flags and bottles of water to receive this young hero. Not because politicians told us to. Because he represented all of us when the state failed him.

The flag @KelvinuhK lifted in Berlin belongs to the people of Kenya, not arrogant bureaucrats sitting in offices weaponizing power against young achievers.

He is not alone we are with him, I'm ready for Tuesday very ready for Tuesday let's meet at the airport.

One of the things the art of photography taught me is the ability to be a critical thinker. Of late traffic jam has been so heavy for example; I reached City Square at 6:45 pm on Monday 10th March 2025 but ended up jumping on a taxi at 8:10 pm.

Reason, the taxis were caught up in the heavy traffic along the way. However, there were a few that came in but were like a hot cake 🤣 and with that the decency I have in me that believes "We shall all eventually reach our destinations" couldn't allow me to join the struggle.

As I waited, I extraordinarily admired the beauty of "Mapeera House" the home of Centenary Bank paying attention to the architecture, blue being one of my favourite colors made it perfect.

NB: PHOTOS WERE TAKEN AFTER 7:30 PM WITH MY PHONE.

The Traffic Jam Crisis: The Hidden Cost to Uganda's Economy — My Daily Mukono–Kampala Ordeal

By Muliira Benon Miles

Living in Mukono while working in Kampala has become an increasingly frustrating experience for thousands of commuters. For many of us, the day begins as early as 4:30 a.m. in an attempt to beat the notorious traffic congestion along the Mukono–Kampala highway. Likewise, many workers are forced to leave their workplaces before 4:00 p.m. simply to avoid spending hours trapped in evening traffic.

Anyone who lives in Mukono or regularly uses the Mukono–Jinja Road understands this reality. What should ordinarily be a manageable commute has become a daily struggle that consumes valuable time, reduces productivity, and negatively affects economic activity.

There is a well-known saying: "Time is money." The phrase reflects the economic reality that time is a limited resource which, when used productively, creates value and generates income. Conversely, wasted time translates into lost opportunities, reduced productivity, and economic costs.

Every morning, I encounter people from all walks of life stuck in traffic: business owners, teachers, doctors, students, tourists, truck drivers transporting cargo, and occasionally even ambulances responding to emergencies. A journey from Kampala to Mukono, a distance of approximately 23 kilometres, should ideally take less than an hour. Yet during peak hours, that same journey can take between three and four hours.

The question then arises: Is time still money when so much of it is lost in traffic congestion?

The Economic Cost of Lost Time

From an economic perspective, traffic congestion represents more than an inconvenience—it is a significant loss of productive resources.

Consider that in some European economies, workers earn substantial hourly wages. According to available wage data, workers in countries such as Bulgaria earn around €8 per hour, while those in Luxembourg earn approximately €47 per hour. Taking an average of these figures gives roughly €27.50 per hour.

If a commuter loses an additional two hours travelling to Kampala and another two hours returning home, that amounts to four unproductive hours lost every day. At an hourly value of €27.50, this would represent a daily loss of €110 per person.

It is estimated that more than 250,000 people use the Mukono–Jinja Road corridor daily. If we apply the same valuation, the cumulative loss amounts to approximately €27.5 million per day, equivalent to roughly UGX 120 billion worth of productive time.

While this illustration is hypothetical and based on international wage comparisons, it highlights the enormous economic value of the time being lost every day due to traffic congestion.

Impact on Tourism and Local Businesses

The consequences extend beyond commuters. Tourists travelling from Kampala to destinations such as Jinja often spend hours on the road instead of enjoying local attractions, restaurants, hotels, and businesses. The longer they remain trapped in traffic, the less time and money they have available to spend at their destinations.

This means local enterprises along the tourism value chain potentially lose revenue, reducing the overall economic benefits that tourism should bring to communities.

A National Development Concern

Traffic congestion on the Mukono–Kampala highway should no longer be viewed merely as a transportation challenge. It is an economic issue that affects productivity, public health, investment, tourism, emergency services, and the quality of life of thousands of Ugandans.

As Uganda continues to pursue economic growth and industrialization, addressing chronic traffic congestion must become a national priority. Investments in road expansion, public transportation systems, alternative transport routes, and urban planning are no longer optional—they are necessary.

Every hour lost in traffic is an hour that could have been spent>>>>

As Uganda's 2026 political season intensifies, 30-year-old boda boda rider Francis Mawejje has announced his presidential bid to challenge President Museveni.

Mawejje says 80% of Uganda’s economy is “hands-to-mouth,” calling it a "boda boda economy" stuck in debt. He decries widespread poverty, youth desperation, and poor public services, claiming the government has turned citizens into beggars. 

His seven-point agenda includes tackling unemployment, improving healthcare and education, reducing bloated government structures, allowing diaspora and prisoners to vote, and trimming presidential powers. “Uganda isn’t poor. Three Police Officers Arrested Over Fatal Shooting of Boda Rider

Police in Kisoro District have arrested three officers following the fatal shooting of a 26-year-old boda boda rider during an operation to recover a motorcycle reportedly stolen from Mbarara.

The victim, identified as Hassan Mwijuke from Gafuruzo Parish, Nyarubuye Sub-county, was shot during an encounter with the officers.

Police say the suspects are in custody as investigations continue, and the firearm believed to have been used in the shooting has been recovered for forensic examination.

Today is World Environment Day, and this year’s theme,  𝘐𝘯𝘴𝘱𝘪𝘳𝘦𝘥 𝘣𝘺 𝘕𝘢𝘵𝘶𝘳𝘦. 𝘍𝘰𝘳 𝘊𝘭𝘪𝘮𝘢𝘵𝘦. 𝘍𝘰𝘳 𝘖𝘶𝘳 𝘍𝘶𝘵𝘶𝘳𝘦 resonates deeply with everyone working to build a cleaner transport future.

At BasiGo, we believe one of the most effective ways to reduce emissions in Africa is through public transport, not because it is the easiest path, but because it moves entire communities and delivers significant impact at scale.

Every electric bus operating across Kenya and Rwanda is proof that clean mobility is no longer a future ambition. It is already transforming how people move today. To date, our buses have driven over 𝟭𝟮 𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘇𝗲𝗿𝗼- 𝗲𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 kilometres across Kenya and Rwanda, connecting people and communities.

Today, let us rethink how we move. From city streets to inter-county routes, zero-emission transport is not only better for the planet but also creates healthier, more sustainable communities for everyone.

#WorldEnvironmentDay

Tweet_Text:
We are proud to have participated in the 4th Annual E-Mobility Stakeholders Conference & Expo 2026, which brought together key industry players advancing electric mobility in Kenya.

Over the two days, policymakers and innovators - including Kenya’s Cabinet Secretary for Energy and Petroleum, Opiyo Wandayi, and Kenya Power Chairperson Joy Brenda Masinde - visited our stand, where Kenya’s first electric school van and an electric van designed for public transport operations were showcased.

Our Managing Director, Moses Nderitu, took the team through both vehicles, highlighting how one shared base model can be adapted to serve different mobility needs across school transport and public service operations.

Across the two days, strong momentum around electric mobility in Kenya was evident, with the sector increasingly taking shape as a scalable, practical solution rather than a future concept.

We are proud to be part of an ecosystem accelerating the transition to clean, efficient transport.

#EMobility2026
#ElectricSchoolVan
#CleanTransport

Tweet_Text:
On 18 May 2026, police shot dead 20 people who were protesting fuel prices — 20 of them in a single day. In the month of May alone, police responded to peaceful protests with live gunfire, killing 24 people in total and injuring tens of others. We still do not know whether the four bodies exhumed from a shallow mass grave in Mwingi were protesters shot by police or victims of extrajudicial killings. No action has been taken against the killer officers or their superiors, who continue sending masked men to shoot protesters.

So far during this month of June, the police have killed three people protesting against Ruto importing Ebola to Kenya, and injured many others. This week, masked men in civilian clothes entered a university campus and shot four students who were protesting the cancellation of their exams. Victor Kariuki and Abubakar Fugicha were admitted in serious condition to Kenyatta National Hospital (KNH) with bullets lodged in their bodies.

In April 2025, the High Court ruled that all police officers deployed to manage protests and public assemblies must be easily identifiable. They are prohibited by law from wearing masks, balaclavas, or civilian clothing and must display visible name tags or service numbers on their official uniforms.

However, Ruto and Murkomen have a group of killers whom they dispatch to peaceful protests, with the sole mission of maiming and killing participants. Protests are not favours to citizens — they are a guaranteed right in the Constitution. You do not need a licence to protest. You only need to notify the police at least three days but not more than 14 days in advance, after which you may protest peacefully.

Since June 2024, however, Ruto has turned protesting into a matter of life and death, with no hesitation in ordering police to shoot people. He's on record telling police to go ahead and shoot people in the leg, take them to hospital and then to court. Rex Masai, one of the first victims of the 2024 anti-finance bill protests, was shot in the leg and died from excessive bleeding after the bullet severed an artery. Ruto's order to shoot people in the legs, not only displayed his complete insensitivity to the loss suffered by Rex's mother, but also sent a chilling message to all mothers that he was willing to kill their children for simply opposing him. He has chosen to violate the Constitution by denying protesting Kenyans their right to life.

In 14 days time, there will be peaceful marches countrywide to mark 25 June — the national holiday when, in 2024, Gen Z protesters entered Parliament and took power back to the people. It is important that we demand an end to this state-sponsored brutality, so that no more families have to bury their sons or daughters because of bloodthirsty police officers targeting young people. 

As we mark 25 June 2026, we must unite to stop our youth from being butchered. 

#RutoStopKillingUs #HakiSASA

Tweet_Text:
Kenya Ports Authority celebrates the arrival of luxury Cruise Liner Norwegian Dawn, to the Port of Mombasa from Seychelles with 2220 tourists aboard.

 With a length overall of 294 metres and a draft of 8.3 metres, she is one of the largest cruise vessels to ever call Mombasa bringing the over 2000 tourists from various nationalities majorly the United States of America to the Kenyan shores. 

Enhanced infrastructure notably a modern cruise terminal and other strategic developments have led to a surge in cruise vessel calls at the ports of Mombasa and Lamu, positioning Kenya as key destination for international cruise liners.

MV Norwegian Dawn sails to Mombasa barely one week since the port hosted two other cruise vessels, MS Europa and MS World Odyssey popularly known as ‘Semester at Sea’.

MV Norwegian Dawn received a special treatment with Roads and Transport Cabinet Secretary witnessing the arrival of the vessel which was also piloted by the KPA Managing Director Capt. William Ruto in the early morning hours when she arrived.

Passengers aboard Norwegian Dawn will enjoy various activities during their one day stay in Mombasa including visiting Tsavo and Amboseli National parks, city excursions and a tour of the wasini island.

Tweet_Text: The National Transport and Safety Authority (NTSA) has officially rolled out a modernized enforcement framework for minor traffic offences under Sections 117 and 117A of the Traffic Act, effective June 1, 2026. Developed alongside the National Police Service and the Judiciary, this digital traffic management system uses a network of over 1,000 smart surveillance cameras across Kenyan highways to detect violations automatically. Instead of immediate roadside arrests or court dates, offenders receive an automated electronic ticket (e-ticket) via SMS or email detailing the violation, time, and specific penalty.
